questions.question: The distance of the point (3, 4, 5) from X-axis is
questions.question_raw: The distance of the point (3, 4, 5) from X-axis is
questions.explanation: The distance of the point (3, 4, 5) from X-axis is: let L be the foot of the perpendicular from the point (3, 4, 5) to X-axis, then coordinate of L will be (3, 0, 0) because on X-axis y and z coordinate are zero then the distance of the point (3, 4, 5) from X-axis i.e. from L (3, 0, 0) is given by {tex}\sqrt {{{(3 - 3)}^2} + {{(4 - 0)}^2} + {{(5 - 0)}^2}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {41} {/tex}

questions.question: The radius of the sphere through the points (4, 3, 0), (0, 4, 3), (0, 5, 0) and (4, 0, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The radius of the sphere through the points (4, 3, 0), (0, 4, 3), (0, 5, 0) and (4, 0, 3) is
questions.explanation:
We know that general equation of sphere is x2 + y2 + z2 + 2gx + 2fy + 2hz + c = 0
Since sphere passing through the points (4, 3, 0), (0, 4, 3), (0, 5, 0) and (4, 0, 3) so putting these values one by one in given equation, we get
16 + 9 + 8g + 6f + c = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8g + 6f + c = -25 ......(i)
16 + 9 + 8f + 6h + c = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8f + 6h + c = -25  ......(ii)
25 + 10f + c  = 0  ......(iii)
16 + 9 + 8g + 6h + c = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8g + 6h + c = -25  ......(iv)
By (ii) - (iv) we have  f = g
Using this in (i) we have 16f +c = -25 .....(v)
Now solving (iii) and (v) we get f = 0 
Using value of f  we have g = 0, h = 0, c = -25
Now radius ={tex}\sqrt {{{( - g)}^2} + ( - f) + {{( - h)}^2} - c} {/tex}  = {tex}\sqrt {25} {/tex} = 5

questions.question: The points (4, 7, 8), (2, 3, 4), (-1, -2, 1) and (1, 2, 5) are the vertices of a
questions.question_raw: The points (4, 7, 8), (2, 3, 4), (-1, -2, 1) and (1, 2, 5) are the vertices of a
questions.explanation:
The vertices of a quadrilateral are A( 4, 7, 8), B(2, 3, 4) ,C (- 1, -2, 1) and D(1, 2, 5) 
AB ={tex}\sqrt {{{(4 - 2)}^2} + {{(7 - 3)}^2} + {{(8 - 4)}^2}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+16+16}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{36}{/tex} = 6 
BC ={tex}\sqrt {{{(2 + 1)}^2} + {{(3 + 2)}^2} + {{(4 - 1)}^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{9+25+9}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{43}{/tex} 
CD ={tex}\sqrt {{{(1 + 1)}^2} + {{(2 + 2)}^2} + {{(5 - 1)}^2}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+16+16}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{36}{/tex} = 6
DA = {tex}\sqrt {{{(4 - 1)}^2} + {{(7 - 2)}^2} + {{(8 - 5)}^2}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{9+25+9}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{43}{/tex} 
Here opposite sides of quadrilateral ABCD are equal, so it may be parallelogram or rectangle
Now , diagonal AC ={tex}\sqrt {{{(4 + 1)}^2} + {{(7 +2)}^2} + {{(8 -1)}^2}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{25+81+49}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{155}{/tex} 
BD = {tex}\sqrt {{{(2 - 1)}^2} + {{(3 - 2)}^2} + {{(4 - 5)}^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1+1+1}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}  
Here the length of diagonals AC and BD are different so ABCD is a parallelogram.

questions.question: The point equidistant from the points (0, 0, 0), (1, 0, 0), (0, 2, 0) and (0, 0, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The point equidistant from the points (0, 0, 0), (1, 0, 0), (0, 2, 0) and (0, 0, 3) is
questions.explanation:
Let the point equidistant from the points (0, 0, 0), (1, 0, 0), (0, 2, 0) and (0, 0, 3) is (x, y, z)
Then according to the given condition and  distance formula between two points we have 
{tex}\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(y-0)^{2}+(z-0)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(x-1)^{2}+(y-0)^{2}+(z-0)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(y-2)^{2}+(z-0)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(y-0)^{2}+(z-3)^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2} + {z^2}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(x-1)^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+(y-2)^{2}+z^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+(z-3)^{2}}{/tex} 
Taking ist two expressions and solving them we get x2 = (x - 1)2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = x2 - 2x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2x + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
Similarly by taking ist and 3rd we get y = 1 and by taking ist and 4th we get z = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} 
So the required point is ({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, 1, {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex})

Co-ordinates of any point on z-axis are (0, 0, z)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The plane x + y = 0 will pass through z-axis if (0, 0, z) satisfies x + y = 0 ....(i)
Now L.H.S of (i) = x + y
For (0, 0, z), L.H.S of (i) = 0 + 0 = 0  = R.H.S of (i)
Hence the result.

questions.question: The centre of the sphere, which passes through (a, 0, 0), (0, b, 0) (0, 0, c ) and (0, 0, 0) is where abc $ \ne $ 0

questions.explanation:
General equation of the sphere is x2 + y2 + z2 + 2gx + 2fy + 2hz + d = 0......(i)
Since (i) passes through the point (0, 0, 0) using this in (i) we get d = 0
Similarly (i) passes through (a, 0, 0), (0, b, 0) (0, 0, c) using these values in (i)
a2 + 2ag = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a(a + 2g) = 0
b2 + 2bf = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b(b + 2f) = 0
c2 + 2ch = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c(c + 2h) = 0
But as abc {tex}\ne {/tex} 0 So, a {tex}\ne {/tex} 0, b {tex}\ne {/tex} 0, c {tex}\ne {/tex} 0
So from above equations  we have a = - 2g, b = - 2f, c = -2h centre is (-f, -g, -h) = ({tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{b}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{c}{2}{/tex})

questions.question: The centre of the sphere through the points ( 0 , 3 , 4 ) , ( 0 , 5 , 0 ) , ( 4 , 0 , 3 ) and ( - 3 , 4 , 0 ) is
questions.question_raw: The centre of the sphere through the points ( 0 , 3 , 4 ) , ( 0 , 5 , 0 ) , ( 4 , 0 , 3 ) and ( - 3 , 4 , 0 ) is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of the required sphere be 
{tex}\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}+2 \mathrm{ux}+2 \mathrm{vy}+2 \mathrm{wz}+\mathrm{d}=0{/tex} ...........(1)
since it passes through (0,3,4),(0,5,0),(4,0,3) and (-3,4,0) therefore
{tex}0^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}+2 u(0)+2 v(3)+2 w(4)+d=0{/tex}
{tex}4^{2}+0^{2}+3^{2}+2 u(4)+2 v(0)+2 w(3)+d=0{/tex}
{tex}(-3)^{2}+4^{2}+0^{2}+2 u(-3)+2 v(4)+2 w(0)+d=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 v+8 w+25+d=0 \ldots \ldots \ldots .(2){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 8 u+6 w+25+d=0 \ldots \ldots \ldots(3){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-6 u+8 v+25+d=0 \ldots \ldots \ldots .(4){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 10 \mathrm{v}+25+\mathrm{d}=0 \ldots \ldots \ldots \text { (5) }{/tex}
Subtracting (5) from (2),(3),(4) respectively, we get 
{tex}\Rightarrow-4 \mathrm{v}+8 \mathrm{w}=0 \ldots \ldots .(6){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 8 u+6 w-10 v=0............(7){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-6 u-2 v=0 \ldots \ldots .(8){/tex}
Solving these, we get, u=v=w=0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Centre of sphere (-u,-v,-w)=(0,0,0)

questions.question: The points A ( 0 , 0 , 0 ) , B ( 1 , $\sqrt 3 $ , 0 ) , C ( 2 , 0 , 0 ) and D ( 1 , 0 , $\sqrt 3 $) are the vertices of
questions.question_raw: The points A ( 0 , 0 , 0 ) , B ( 1 , {tex}\sqrt 3 {/tex} , 0 ) , C ( 2 , 0 , 0 ) and D ( 1 , 0 , {tex}\sqrt 3 {/tex}) are the vertices of
questions.explanation:
Direction ratios of AB are {tex}(1-0, \sqrt{3}-0,0-0) \text { i.e }(1, \sqrt{3}, 0){/tex}
Direction ratios of BC are{tex}(1-0, \sqrt{3}-0,0-0) \text { i.e }(1,-\sqrt{3}, 0){/tex}
Direction ratios of CD are {tex}(-1-0,0-0, \sqrt{3}-0) \text { i.e }(-1,0, \sqrt{3}){/tex}
Direction ratios of CD are {tex}(-1-0,0-0,-\sqrt{3}-0) \text { i.e }(-1,0, \sqrt{3}){/tex}
If ABCD is a parallelogram, then
{tex}\mathrm{AB} \| \mathrm{CD} \text { and } \mathrm{AD} \| \mathrm{BC}{/tex}
Now,{tex}\frac{1}{-1} \neq \frac{\sqrt{3}}{0} \neq \frac{0}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because\frac{{\mathbf a}_1}{{\mathbf a}_2}\neq\frac{\mathbf b1}{{\mathbf b}_2}\neq\frac{{\mathbf c}_1}{{\mathbf c}_2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB is not parallel to CD
Similarly {tex}\frac{-1}{1} \neq \frac{0}{\sqrt{-3}} \neq \frac{-\sqrt{3}}{0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AD and BC are not parallel 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ABCD is not a parallelogram and hence it is not a square or rhombus

questions.question: If the line $\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{-1}$ intersects the plane 2x + 3 y - z + 13 = 0 at a point P and the plane 3x + y + 4z = 16 at a point Q, then PQ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the line {tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{-1}{/tex} intersects the plane 2x + 3 y - z + 13 = 0 at a point P and the plane 3x + y + 4z = 16 at a point Q, then PQ is equal to
questions.explanation:
Equation of given line is
{tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{-1}=r(\operatorname{let}){/tex} ...(i)
Now, coordinates of a general point over given
line is R(3r + 2 ,2r - 1, - r + 1)
Let the coordinates of point P are (3r1 + 2, 2r1 - 1, r1 + 1) and Q are (3r2 + 2, 2r2 - 1, - r2 + 1).
Since, P is the point of intersection of line (i) and the
plane 2x + 3y - z + 13 = 0, so
2(3r1 + 2) + 3(2r1 - 1) - (-r1 + 1) + 13 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6r1 + 4 + 6r1 - 3 + r1 - 1 + 13 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 13r1 + 13 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r1 = -1
So, point P (-1, - 3, 2)
And, similarly for point ‘Q', we get
3(3r2 + 2) + (2r2 - 1) + 4(-r2+ 1) = 16
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7r2 = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 1
So, point is Q (5, 1, 0)
Now, {tex}P Q=\sqrt{(5+1)^{2}+(1+3)^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{36+16+4}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{56}=2 \sqrt{14}{/tex}

questions.question: Two-line $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-6}{-1}$ and $\frac{x+5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4}$ intersect at the point R. The reflection of R in the xy-plane has coordinates
questions.question_raw: Two-line {tex}\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-6}{-1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} intersect at the point R. The reflection of R in the xy-plane has coordinates
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\frac{x-3}{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y+1}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{z-6}{-1}{/tex} = a (say). Then, any point on this line is of form P(a + 3, 3a -1, -a + 6)
Similarly, any point on the line. {tex}\frac{x+5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} = b (say), is of the form Q(7b - 5, -6b + 2, 4b + 3)
Now, if the lines are intersect, then P = Q for some a and b.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + 3 = 7b - 5
3a - 1 = -6b + 2 and -a + 6 = 4b + 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - 7b = -8, a + 2b = 1 and a + 4b = 3
On solving a - 7b = -8 and a + 2b = 1, we get b = 1 and a = -1, which also satisfy a + 4b = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P = Q {tex}\equiv{/tex} (2, -4, 7) for a = -1 and b = 1
Thus, coordinates of point R are (2, -4, 7) and reflection of R in xy-plane is (2, -4, -7)

questions.question: The plane containing the line $\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{3}$ and also containing its projection on the plane 2x + 3y - z = 5, contains which one of the following points?
questions.question_raw: The plane containing the line {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex} and also containing its projection on the plane 2x + 3y - z = 5, contains which one of the following points?
questions.explanation:
Let the direction vector of the line
{tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex} is {tex}{b}=2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}{/tex}
Since, the required plane contains this line and its projection along the plane 2x+ 3y - z = 5, it will also contain the normal of the plane 2x + 3y - z = 5.

Normal vector of the plane 2x+ 3y - z = b is {tex}\vec{n}=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}{/tex}
Now, the required plane contains {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{n}=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Normal of the required plane is {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{n}{/tex}.
Since the plane contains the line
{tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex}, therefore it also contains the point
{tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{{i}}-2 \hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}{/tex}.
Now, the equation of the required plane is {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{n})=0{/tex}
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {x-3} & {y+2} & {z-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \\ {2} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right|=0 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(x-3)[1-9]-(y+2)[-2-6]+(z-1)[6+2]=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-8 x+8 y+8 z+32=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x  y - z = 4
Note that (2, 0, - 2) is the only point which satisfies the above equation.

questions.question: On which of the following lines lies the point of intersection of the line, $\frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y-5}{2}=\frac{z-3}{1}$ and the plane, x + y + z = 2?
questions.question_raw: On which of the following lines lies the point of intersection of the line, {tex}\frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y-5}{2}=\frac{z-3}{1}{/tex} and the plane, x + y + z = 2?
questions.explanation:
Given equation of line is
{tex}\frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y-5}{2}=\frac{z-3}{1}=r(\operatorname{let}){/tex} ......(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2r + 4; y = 2r + 5 and z = r + 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} General point on the line (i) is P(2r + 4, 2r + 5, r + 3)
So, the point of intersection of line (i) and plane x + y + z = 2 will be of the form P(2r + 4, 2r + 5, r + 3) for some {tex}r \in R{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2r + 4) + (2r + 5) + (r + 3) = 2  [{tex}\because{/tex} the point will lie on the plane]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5r = -10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = -2
So, the point of intersection is P(0, 1, 1) [Putting r = -2 in (2r + 4, 2r + 5, r + 3)]
Now, on checking the options, we get
{tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+4}{-5}{/tex} contain the point (0, 1, 1).

questions.question_raw: A(3, 2, 0), B (5, 3, 2) and C( -9, 6, -3) are the vertices of a triangle ABC. If the bisector of ABC meets BC at D, then coordinates of D are

AD is bisector of {tex}\angle B A C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ratio at D is c : b, where
c = AB = {tex}\sqrt{(3-5)^{2}+(2-3)^{2}+(0-2)^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}{/tex} = 3
b = AC = {tex}\sqrt{(3+9)^{2}+(2-6)^{2}+(0+3)^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{144+16+9}=\sqrt{169}{/tex} = 13

{tex}\therefore{/tex} For point D:
x = {tex}\frac{c(-9)+b(5)}{c+b}=\frac{3(-9)+13(5)}{3+13}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{38}{16}=\frac{19}{8}{/tex}
y = {tex}\frac{c(6)+b(3)}{c+b}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3(6)+13(3)}{3+13}=\frac{57}{16}{/tex}
z = {tex}\frac{c(-3)+b(2)}{c+b}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3(-3)+13(2)}{3+13}=\frac{17}{16}{/tex}
Hence, point D is {tex}\left(\frac{19}{8}, \frac{57}{16}, \frac{17}{6}\right){/tex}

Suppose d is the diameter of the sphere. 
then d = 
d = {tex}\sqrt{(-1 - 3)^2 + (2 - 4)^2 + (3 + 1)^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d2 = (-1 - 3)2 + (2 - 4)2 + (3 + 1)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d2 = (-4)2 + (-2)2 + (4)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d2 = 16 + 4 + 16
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d2 = 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac62{/tex} = 3
Hence, the radius of the sphere is 3 units.

questions.question: The point equidistant from the points O (0, 0, 0), P (2, 0, 0), Q (0, 4, 0) and R (0, 0, 6) is
questions.question_raw: The point equidistant from the points O (0, 0, 0), P (2, 0, 0), Q (0, 4, 0) and R (0, 0, 6) is
questions.explanation:
Let S = (a, b, c) be the point equidistant from the given points. Then
OS2 = PS2 = QS2 = RS2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 + c2 = (a - 2)2 + b2 + c2
= a2 + (b - 4)2 + c2
= a2 + b2 + (c - 6)2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -4a + 4 = 0, -8b + 16 = 0, -12c + 36 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a = 1, b = 2, c = 3
Required point = (1, 2, 3)

questions.question: If the sum of the squares of the distance of a point from the three co-ordinates axes is 48, then its distance from the origin is
questions.question_raw: If the sum of the squares of the distance of a point from the three co-ordinates axes is 48, then its distance from the origin is
questions.explanation:
According to the given condition,
{tex}\left(\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\sqrt{y^{2}+z^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\sqrt{z^{2}+x^{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} = 48
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(x2 + y2 + z2) = 48 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 + z2 = 24
The distance of the point P (x, y, z) from the origin is {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}=\sqrt{24}=2 \sqrt{6}{/tex}

questions.question: If P be a point, the sum of squares of whose distances from the planes x + z = 0 and z - x = 0 is 16 units, then the locus of point P is:
questions.question_raw: If P be a point, the sum of squares of whose distances from the planes x + z = 0 and z - x = 0 is 16 units, then the locus of point P is:
questions.explanation:
Let P = {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex}
Given condition
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{|\alpha+\gamma|^{2}}{2}+\frac{|\gamma-\alpha|^{2}}{2}{/tex} = 16
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{2}+\gamma^{2}{/tex} = 16
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The locus of point P is x2 + z2 = 16

questions.question: A point moves such that the sum of the squares of its distances from the six faces of a cube given by x = $\pm2$, y = $\pm2$, z = $\pm2$ is 28 units. The locus of the point is:
questions.question_raw: A point moves such that the sum of the squares of its distances from the six faces of a cube given by x = {tex}\pm2{/tex}, y = {tex}\pm2{/tex}, z = {tex}\pm2{/tex} is 28 units. The locus of the point is:
questions.explanation:
Let the moving point be ({tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex}, {tex}\gamma{/tex}).
Given condition
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{(\alpha-2)^{2}}{1}+\frac{(\alpha+2)^{2}}{1}+\frac{(\beta-2)^{2}}{1}+\frac{(\beta+2)^{2}}{1} + \frac{(\gamma-2)^{2}}{1}+\frac{(\gamma+2)^{2}}{1}{/tex} = 28
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2({tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + {tex}\beta^2{/tex} + {tex}\gamma^2{/tex}) + 24 = 28
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2({tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + {tex}\beta^2{/tex} + {tex}\gamma^2{/tex}) = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + {tex}\beta^2{/tex} + {tex}\gamma^2{/tex}) = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The locus of the point is x2 + y2 + z2 = 2

questions.question: The length of the perpendicular drawn from the point (4, -7, 3) on the y-axis is:
questions.question_raw: The length of the perpendicular drawn from the point (4, -7, 3) on the y-axis is:
questions.explanation:
Given point is (4, -7, 3)
Its foot of perpendicular on y-axis is (0, -7, 0)
Distance between two points is
{tex}\sqrt{(4-0)^2+(-7-(-7))^2+(3-0)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{4^2+3^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex}
= 5 units

questions.question: The foot of perpendicular from the origin O to a plane P which meets the co-ordinate axes at the points A, B, C is $(2, a, 4), a \in N$. If the volume of the tetrahedron OABC is 144 unit^3, then which of the following points is NOT on P?
questions.question_raw: The foot of perpendicular from the origin O to a plane P which meets the co-ordinate axes at the points A, B, C is {tex}(2, a, 4), a \in N{/tex}. If the volume of the tetrahedron OABC is 144 unit3, then which of the following points is NOT on P?
questions.explanation:
Equation of Plane :
{tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{{a}}) \cdot \vec{{n}}=0{/tex}
{tex}(2 \hat{i}+a \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \cdot[(x-2) \hat{i}{/tex} {tex}+(y-a) \hat{j}+(z-4) \hat{k}]=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  2x + ay + 4z = 20 + a2
Since plane P intercept the coordinate axes at A, B and C
{tex} \begin{aligned} \therefore A & \equiv\left(\frac{20+\mathrm{a}^{2}}{2}, 0,0\right), \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} \mathrm{B} \equiv\left(0, \frac{20+\mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}}, 0\right), \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{C} \equiv\left(0,0, \frac{20+\mathrm{a}^{2}}{4}\right) {/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Volume of tetrahedron {tex}=\frac{1}{6}[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}]=\frac{1}{6} \vec{a} \cdot[\vec{b} \times \vec{c}]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{20+a^{2}}{2}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{20+a^{2}}{a}\right) {/tex}{tex}\cdot\left(\frac{20+a^{2}}{4}\right)=144 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (20 + a2)3 = 144 {tex}\times{/tex} 48 {tex}\times{/tex} a = (12)3 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 22 {tex}\cdot{/tex} a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  a = 2 
So, equation of plane is 2x + 2y + 4z = 24
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y + 2z = 12
We observe from option that (3, 0, 4) not lies on the Plane x + y + 2z = 12

Let S be the set of all four lines containing the main diagonals of the cube Q; for instance, the line passing through the vertices (0, 0, 0) and (1, 1, 1) is in S. Let S be the set of all four lines containing the main diagonals of the cube Q; for instance, the line passing through the vertices (0, 0, 0) and (1, 1, 1) is in S. Then the maximum value of d  ({tex}l_1{/tex}, {tex}l_2{/tex}),  as {tex}l_1{/tex} varies over F and {tex}l_2{/tex} varies over S, is
questions.explanation:
Equation of face diagonal OD line is

{tex}l_2: \vec{r}=\hat{j}+\mu(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
Shortest distance = {tex}\left|\frac{j \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}) \times(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})}{(\hat{i}+\hat{j}) \times(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})}\right|{/tex}

questions.question: Let P be a plane $l x+m y+n z=0$ containing the line, $\frac{1-x}{1}=\frac{y+4}{2}=\frac{z+2}{3}$. If plane P divides the line segment AB joining points ${A}(-3,-6,1)$ and ${B}(2,4,-3)$ in ratio $k: 1$ then the value of $k$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let P be a plane {tex}l x+m y+n z=0{/tex} containing the line, {tex}\frac{1-x}{1}=\frac{y+4}{2}=\frac{z+2}{3}{/tex}. If plane P divides the line segment AB joining points {tex}{A}(-3,-6,1){/tex} and {tex}{B}(2,4,-3){/tex} in ratio {tex}k: 1{/tex} then the value of {tex}k{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given; {tex}{A}(-3,-6,1), {B}(2,4,-3){/tex}
Plane P divide the line segment AB in the ratio {tex}k: 1{/tex}
C{tex}\left(\frac{2 k-3}{k+1}, \frac{4 k-6}{k+1}, \frac{-3 k+1}{k+1}\right){/tex}
Equation of line {tex}\frac{1-x}{1}=\frac{y+4}{2}=\frac{z+2}{3}{/tex} satisfied to the plane then, {tex}l x+m y+n z=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow l(-1)+m(2)+n(3)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-l+2 m+3 n=0{/tex} ...(ii)
Since, {tex}l x+m y+n z=0{/tex} also satisfy point {tex}(1,-4,-2){/tex}
Then, {tex}l-4 m-2 n=0{/tex} ...(iii)
Now using (ii) and (iii)
{tex}n=2 m{/tex}
{tex}l=8 m{/tex}
{tex}\frac{l}{8}=\frac{m}{1}=\frac{n}{2}{/tex}
{tex}l: m: n=8: 1: 2{/tex}
Equation of plane will be {tex}8 x+y+2 z=0{/tex}
Point C will satisfy {tex}8 x+y+2 z=0{/tex}, then
{tex}8\left(\frac{2 k-3}{k+1}\right)+\left(\frac{4 k-6}{k+1}\right)+2\left(\frac{-3 k+1}{k+1}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 16 k-24+4 k-6-6 k+2=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow k=2{/tex}

questions.question: Let the vertices $Q$ and $R$ of the triangle $P Q R$ lie on the line $\frac{x+3}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+4}{3}, {QR}$ $=5$ and the coordinates of the point P be $(0,2,3)$. If the area of the triangle $P Q R$ is $\frac{m}{n}$ then:
questions.question_raw: Let the vertices {tex}Q{/tex} and {tex}R{/tex} of the triangle {tex}P Q R{/tex} lie on the line {tex}\frac{x+3}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+4}{3}, {QR}{/tex} {tex}=5{/tex} and the coordinates of the point P be {tex}(0,2,3){/tex}. If the area of the triangle {tex}P Q R{/tex} is {tex}\frac{m}{n}{/tex} then:
questions.explanation:
{tex}{QR} =5{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x+3}{5} =\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+4}{3}=\lambda{/tex}
Any point on line L is: {tex}5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda-4{/tex}
Let, {tex}{M}=5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda-4{/tex}
Drs of {tex}{PM} \Rightarrow 5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda-7{/tex}
Drs of line {tex}{L} \Rightarrow 5,2,3{/tex}
L and PM are perpendicular,
{tex}\therefore 5(5 \lambda-3)+2(2 \lambda-1)+3(3 \lambda-7)=0{/tex}
{tex}25 \lambda-15+4 \lambda-2+9 \lambda-21=0{/tex}
{tex}38 \lambda-38=0{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {M}=(2,3,-1){/tex}
{tex} \text { PM }=\sqrt{4+1+16}=\sqrt{21}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Area } =\frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times \sqrt{21}=\frac{m}{n}{/tex}
{tex}2 m-5 \sqrt{21} n=0{/tex}
